title: Huawei Wins China's AI Chip War as Chemical Export Bans Reshape Supply Chains
subhead: US export controls accidentally built Huawei's domestic AI chip monopoly, while China's quiet dominance in sulphuric acid exports gives Beijing a new lever over global fertiliser and battery supply chains—revealing how industrial policy and mineral processing have become the real battleground of tech power.

sections.heading: Huawei's $12 Billion AI Chip Win
sections.body:
Huawei expects AI chip revenue to hit $12 billion in 2026, a 60% jump from $7.5 billion last year, driven by domestic demand that US export controls on Nvidia accidentally created. When Washington barred Nvidia from selling advanced GPUs to China, it didn't eliminate Chinese AI compute—it handed the market to Huawei.

The Ascend 950 chip, running DeepSeek's V4 model, has become the default choice for Chinese tech firms suddenly cut off from Nvidia. Major internet companies are scrambling to secure orders, creating a seller's market for Huawei's AI silicon. The chip isn't as powerful as Nvidia's best, but it's available—and for Chinese AI firms facing US sanctions, availability now beats performance.

This is the exact opposite of what export controls were supposed to do. The policy goal was to slow Chinese AI development by denying access to cutting-edge compute. Instead, it accelerated China's indigenous chip industry and created a captive domestic market for Huawei. The company that Western governments spent years trying to exclude from 5G networks is now the primary beneficiary of semiconductor sanctions.
sections.whyItMatters: US export controls have backfired at scale, creating a $12B domestic AI chip monopoly for Huawei while failing to slow Chinese AI development. The policy inadvertently de-risked Huawei from foreign competition within its home market.

sections.heading: China's Sulphuric Acid Export Ban Exposes Hidden Supply Chain Leverage
sections.body:
China is reportedly banning sulphuric acid exports starting in May, removing 3 million tonnes from global markets—about 23% of worldwide exports. The chemical is critical for fertiliser production, copper and nickel mining, and lead-acid batteries. Beijing has quietly made itself a linchpin in a commodity most policymakers never thought about, and now it's weaponising that dependency.

The ban comes amid the Iran-Israel war that has already disrupted Middle Eastern supply through the Strait of Hormuz. China's move compounds the squeeze. Prices have already doubled since January. For countries like Chile, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, Morocco and India—all major importers—the shortage will cascade into fertiliser costs, food prices, and mining output.

China's Ministry of Commerce hasn't officially confirmed the ban, but suppliers have already notified international buyers. The restriction is expected to last through at least the end of 2026. Western equipment manufacturers are now asking whether magnesium will be next—China already produces most of the world's supply and needs it for lightweight vehicle manufacturing.

This is industrial policy as strategic leverage. China has spent years building processing capacity for critical minerals and chemicals. Now that processing dominance is becoming a foreign policy tool.
sections.whyItMatters: China's dominance in industrial chemical processing gives Beijing leverage over global food production (fertiliser), mining operations (copper/nickel extraction), and battery manufacturing. This is rare earth export controls 2.0—more pervasive and harder to substitute.

sections.heading: Iran-Linked Hackers Turn Psychological Against US Troops
sections.body:
The Handala hacking group, believed to be affiliated with Iranian intelligence, has shifted tactics from infrastructure attacks to direct psychological warfare against US military personnel. Iranian-aligned hackers sent threatening WhatsApp messages to American service members at bases in the Persian Gulf, warning they would be targeted by missiles and drones. “Your identities are fully known to our missile units,” one message read. “Very soon, you will be targeted by our Shahed drones.”

The messages targeted troops in Bahrain, home to the US Fifth Fleet. The group claimed to have compromised personal data on thousands of service members. While some leaked phone numbers appeared incomplete or outdated, the fact that Handala successfully messaged personnel directly—reaching them on personal devices in a war zone—marks an escalation in hybrid warfare.

This is no longer about stealing data or disrupting systems. The objective, according to cyber intelligence analysts, is now “intimidation, influence, and creating fear among the deployed personnel, or potentially their families.” The breach of FBI Director Kash Patel's personal Gmail in March showed the group's capability. The shift to psychological targeting shows how they're using it.

The Navy has responded by urging sailors to lock down their digital footprints, warning about dating apps and personal photo sharing. The directive acknowledges that modern warfare includes the personal device in every sailor's pocket.
sections.whyItMatters: Cyber operations have merged with psychological warfare. Iranian proxies are now using hacked data to intimidate US troops directly, bypassing institutional systems to target individuals. This represents a new form of hybrid warfare where personal data becomes ammunition.

sections.heading: ICE Uses Facial Recognition to Track US Citizens Observing Federal Agents
sections.body:
Federal immigration agents are using facial recognition technology to build a database of US citizens who observe and document enforcement operations, according to a federal court declaration filed in Minnesota. An ICE agent approached Nicole Cleland, a Target Corporation director who was following agents to observe their actions, addressed her by name, and told her he was recording her with facial recognition technology. Three days later, her Global Entry and TSA PreCheck status was revoked.

The ACLU of Minnesota says this is part of an expanding pattern. Federal agents are increasingly using facial recognition not just for enforcement targets, but to identify, record, and apparently retaliate against citizens exercising First Amendment rights to observe government activity. The technology, called Mobile Fortify, was developed under the Trump Administration and queries CBP immigration datasets.

DHS denies the tool scrapes social media or uses publicly available data, insisting it operates under established legal authorities. But the ACLU argues the issue isn't the recording itself—it's the threatened retaliation and the actual punishment. Cleland stated she has not engaged in further legal observation of federal agents since the incident. The government, she said, is “successfully dissuading people from exercising their constitutional rights.”
sections.whyItMatters: Surveillance technology designed for border enforcement is now being used to monitor and deter domestic political activity. The same tools exported to authoritarian regimes are being deployed against US citizens at home, blurring the line between national security and domestic control.

sections.heading: Chinese State Hackers Targeted Japan's Security and Semiconductor Sectors
sections.body:
Japan's National Police Agency has publicly identified the Chinese hacker group “MirrorFace” as responsible for cyberattacks on 210 Japanese organisations since 2019, including the Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA), the Foreign Ministry, Defense Ministry, Cabinet Secretariat, and LDP lawmakers. More than 10,000 files may have been leaked from JAXA alone. The NPA believes MirrorFace is related to APT10, a group affiliated with China's Ministry of State Security.

The attacks used targeted emails with subject lines referencing “Japan-US Alliance,” “Taiwan Strait,” and “Russia-Ukraine War”—topics calculated to attract attention from defence and foreign policy officials. The group exploited VPN vulnerabilities and used malware to infiltrate networks. Targets spanned semiconductors, manufacturing, aerospace, think tanks, and academia.

Japan is now preparing an “active cyberdefense” bill that would allow the government to collect and analyse online communications to prevent attacks before they occur. The legislation faces constitutional questions about the secrecy of communications, but the scale of the Chinese targeting has created political momentum for expanded government cyber powers.
sections.whyItMatters: China's systematic targeting of Japan's semiconductor and aerospace sectors shows how cyber espionage serves industrial policy. The attacks map directly to Beijing's strategic priorities—technology transfer from a key US ally.

sections.heading: TSMC Ramps 2nm Production as US-China Chip War Creates Two-Tier Market
sections.body:
TSMC has begun mass production at five 2nm fabrication plants in the fastest capacity expansion in the company's history, targeting AI and high-performance computing demand. The simultaneous ramp-up across multiple facilities represents a major leap over 3nm capacity. But the global semiconductor market is now irrevocably split: TSMC's most advanced nodes flow to Western and allied customers, while Huawei's Ascend chips serve China's captive domestic market.

The AI data centre boom is consuming so much advanced silicon that consumer electronics manufacturers are reporting chip shortages. The same production capacity that once served smartphones and laptops is now dedicated to AI accelerators. For companies without the priority status of Nvidia, Apple, or major cloud providers, lead times are extending.

TSMC's expansion keeps Taiwan at the centre of advanced manufacturing—for now. But the US CHIPS Act subsidies and Japan's own fab investments are gradually diversifying production geography. The question is whether diversification happens fast enough to matter in a conflict scenario, or whether Taiwan's concentration of cutting-edge production remains the single point of failure in global tech supply chains.
sections.whyItMatters: TSMC's 2nm ramp shows Taiwan maintaining its technical edge, but the market is fragmenting into US-allied and China-isolated spheres. Each side is building independent supply chains—a slow-motion decoupling with implications for any future Taiwan conflict.

sections.heading: China's 'Coal Battery' Turns Fossil Fuel Into Containment Problem
sections.body:
Chinese researchers at Shenzhen University have demonstrated a “coal battery” that generates electricity from coal without combustion, feeding carbon through a sealed electric cell. The technology reframes coal power as a containment problem: the dirtiest fuel can produce current without sending exhaust into the atmosphere, potentially enabling carbon capture at the source.

The zero-carbon-emission direct coal fuel cell (ZC-DCFC) operates at 1,100 to 1,650 degrees Fahrenheit, converting coal's carbon directly into electricity through chemical reactions rather than heat. Carbon dioxide exits as a concentrated stream, making capture, storage, or conversion easier than with diluted smokestack emissions.

China still draws 60% of its electricity from coal and commissioned 78 gigawatts of new coal capacity in 2025. The coal battery doesn't eliminate that dependency, but it could change the emissions equation. The researchers propose siting the cells underground in mines, storing carbon near the coal seams. Commercial viability remains unproven—small cells work in labs, but full-scale power stations need to run reliably for years while handling ash, sulfur, and thermal stress.

If the technology scales, it gives China a pathway to use its massive coal reserves while meeting climate commitments—a strategic option other coal-dependent nations don't have.
sections.whyItMatters: If China solves coal emissions through technology rather than phase-out, it retains a strategic energy advantage while appearing climate-compliant. This would fundamentally alter the geopolitics of fossil fuel transitions.

bottomLine: The single most important tech-power shift this week is China's demonstration that it can weaponise processing dominance in obscure industrial chemicals—sulphuric acid—to pressure global food and battery supply chains. Combined with Huawei's $12 billion AI chip windfall from US sanctions and TSMC's 2nm expansion for allied markets, we're witnessing the solidification of two largely independent technology ecosystems. The US and its allies control advanced manufacturing; China controls processing and refining for the inputs those systems require. Neither side can fully decouple, but both are building the capacity to survive the other's absence. The question for 2026 is whether this mutual dependency creates stability or accelerates conflict.
sources: Reuters, South China Morning Post, Yahoo News/Task & Purpose, KSTP Minneapolis, Asahi Shimbun, Earth.com, TNW, ACLU of Minnesota, Japan National Police Agency, Argus Media, Observatory of Economic Complexity
